query: erabutoxin b source and natural occurrence in plants

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In certain cases, one alkaloid may serve as the antidote for the toxicity of another. Since the discovery of morphine by modern chemistry, through repurposing and metabolic engineering, plant alkaloids have been exploited for their immense medicinal properties in a wealth of pharmacologic applications. These include the treatment of pain (e.g., morphine, cocaine), fever (e.g., quinine), malignancy (e.g., vinblastine, berberine), asthma (e.g., ephedrine), dysrhythmias (e.g., quinidine), hyperglycemia (e.g., piperine), hypertension (e.g., reserpine), and bacterial infections (e.g., ciprofloxacin). This activity will incorporate the available data to highlight the less desirable effects of these metabolites, the exposure to which may lead to disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Glycoside and Digoxin Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Epidemiologic data on toxicity from oleandrin and bufalin is less robust. Oleandrin toxicity is seen more commonly in tropical and subtropical climates, where Nerium oleander and Thevetia peruviana growth is more pronounced. Unintentional ingestion by children has been seen, as well as intentional suicidal gestures in adults, which is more common in India and Sri Lanka. Yellow oleander is also used in traditional medicine, which can lead to toxicity with inaccurate dosing. The plant is part of the natural ecosystem in the United States, particularly in the Southwest. Yellow oleander is also grown as an ornamental plant, but toxicity is rare due to its bitter taste.

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The more serious toxicities tend to involve adults who may mistake the plant in question as edible or intentionally ingest it for its homeopathic or toxic properties. Fortunately, only about 7% of individuals require medical evaluation, less than 20% report mild to moderate symptoms, and over 80% are asymptomatic. The fatality rate of such exposures in the United States, largely unintentional, is less than 0.001%. In other parts of the world where health care is less accessible, however, plant exposures pose a greater risk and public health burden. Between 1983 and 2009, only 45 fatalities were recorded, with Datura and Cicuta species accounting for 35.5% of these. Between 2000 and 2009, 52.2% of toxic plant ingestions involved males, and over 60% of such cases resulted in moderate to severe outcomes. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Plant chemistry and toxicology are complex with a rich history. Much about plant chemistry remains unknown, and our understanding relies on animal research combined with documented human experiences. The dynamic science of pharmacognosy, which highlights the therapeutic value of plants, has elucidated several major classes of organic molecules found in plants, of which alkaloids represent one. Others include phenols and phenylpropanoids; terpenes and resins; glycosides; and proteins, peptides, and lectins. Plant alkaloids represent a diverse array of secondary plant metabolites exemplified by familiar compounds such as nicotine, caffeine, cocaine, mescaline, ephedrine, and strychnine. Chemically classified as amines, plant alkaloids are defined by their function as bases generally containing one or more nitrogen atoms within a heterocyclic structure. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In many instances, as in the case of nicotinic toxicity, only small concentrations of a toxin may be required to cause illness. Poison hemlock has been unintentionally ingested by foragers searching for wild carrots due to the similar appearance of the two plants. Cytisine, another nicotine-like alkaloid found in Cytisus laburnum , has been held responsible for the mass poisoning of children and adults who consumed as little as 0.5 milligrams (mg) per kilogram (kg) body weight.

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Plant alkaloids represent a widely diverse group of secondary plant metabolites found ubiquitously in nature and increasingly in households through indoor plants. The science of pharmacognosy has uncovered the equally diverse therapeutic potential of these phytochemicals. Conversely, they represent the toxic components of many of the well-known poisonous plants. Both humans and animals may be exposed to these phytotoxins and experience noxious consequences. The clinical effects that may ensue may range from ambiguous to illustrations of classic toxidromes. A thorough history and physical examination are foremost in identifying the diagnosis and thus in dictating management. This activity outlines the evaluation and management of plant alkaloid toxicity and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

and(B) had a pre-existing provider-patient relationship with an advanced practice registered nurselicensed under Title 58, Chapter 31b, Nurse Practice Act, a physician licensed under Title58, Chapter 67, Utah Medical Practice Act, a physician licensed under Title 58, Chapter68, Utah Osteopathic Medical Practice Act, or a physician assistant licensed under TitleUtah CodePage 858, Chapter 70a, Utah Physician Assistant Act, who believed that the individual's illnessdescribed in Subsection (2)(a)(i)(A) could benefit from the use in question;(ii) for possession, was:(A) the parent or legal guardian of an individual described in Subsection (2)(a)(i) who is a minor; or(B) the spouse of an individual described in Subsection (2)(a)(i); or(iii)(A) for possession, was a medical cannabis cardholder; or(B) for use, was a medical cannabis patient cardholder or a minor with a qualifying condition under the supervision of a medical cannabis guardian cardholder; retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Commonly, patients will experience amnesia to events following ingestion. Important to note is that not all characteristics of this toxidrome will necessarily be present in every case. These plant metabolites have been exploited for their hallucinogenic properties recreationally via the consumption of brewed teas and seeds. Plants containing these phytochemicals from the family Solanaceae may also be smoked. Datura stramonium (jimsonweed), the chief source of such seeds, is found in the United States and Southern Canada, and poisoning may lead to acute anticholinergic poisoning and death in both children and adults. Recreational ingestions exceed unintentional ingestions as the main cause of toxicity. The duration of the effect of belladonna poisoning may last from a few hours to days, depending on the dose of exposure. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Beeturia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Studies of individuals with higher rates of beeturia than the general population suggest that in the state of iron deficiency or other states of high iron absorption in the GI system, the betacyanins had a higher uptake, and as a result, presented in the urine. [4] Other studies linked betacyanin uptake to GI absorption with a specific focus on pH variance in the stomach and small bowel, without hepatic or renal clearance having any found links. [2] Also in consideration are co-ingestions such as oxalic acid-containing compounds such as spinach or rhubarb, as these may increase absorption of the betacyanin alkaloid. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Plant alkaloid toxicity results from over-exposure to plants harboring these phytochemicals, including seeds, flowers, or weeds, in the form of extracts, tinctures, herbal teas, or as contaminants of food products. Utilization may be inadvertent or deliberate- recreational or for self-harm. Contact may be direct or indirect through inhalation, ingestion, transdermal, or vertical transmission. Nicotine toxicity can result after ingestion of Nicotiana tabacum leaves, insecticidal products, electronic cigarette refills, cigarettes, and transdermal, as in green tobacco sickness affecting farm workers who harvest tobacco. Pyrrolizidine alkaloid toxicity may occur secondary to ingestion of parent plants for medicinal purposes or via consumption of food grain contaminated with the seeds of such plants. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although the data related to alkaloid-specific exposures are limited, those involving general plant exposures are well-documented. In the United States, plant-related toxic exposures exceed one-hundred-thousand cases annually and account for about 3 to 5% of human exposures described to poison control centers. The most common route of exposure is ingestion, with approximately 98% of these being unintentional. Nearly 80% of reported plant-related poisonings involve children under six years of age. Most of the plants responsible for these events have limited toxicity, and most exposures are benign. Moreover, pediatric ingestions tend to be benign owing to the small quantities consumed. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Botulism -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most cases of botulism during this time frame in the United States were due to home-canning of foods; in Europe, they were primarily due to commercial foods. Toxin A was the cause of 58% of the outbreaks in the United States, 45% in Europe, and 50% in Asia, whereas toxin type E was responsible for 50% of Canadian outbreaks. [17] The incubation period was shorter in toxin type E botulism than in cases caused by toxin A; case-patients involving type A toxin had the highest mean percentage of mechanical ventilation, and mortality was lower for outbreaks in which all cases with toxin A and E botulism had received matching antitoxin (7.8% vs 53.9%; P < .001). [15] These findings are consistent with other reports. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Deadly Single Dose Agents -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Plant toxin/secondary metabolite - colchicine

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Isoquinoline alkaloids include structurally similar sanguinarine, hydrastine, and berberine. Berberine has demonstrated cardiac and respiratory depression, smooth muscle contraction, and uterine contraction. A mass sanguinarine poisoning from the consumption of a Mexican prickly poppy ( Argemone mexicana ) resulted in gastrointestinal symptoms, hepatomegaly, anemia, skin darkening and lesions, erythema, and peripheral edema. On the severe end, affected individuals may experience ascites, congestive heart failure, and myocarditis. Hydrastine ingestion has been reported to cause movement disorders that resemble poisoning by a non-isoquinoline alkaloid, strychnine, found in Strychnos nux-vomica. Poisoning from these alkaloids is associated with muscle spasms, weakness, rigidity, and death via respiratory muscle involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: Butterbur -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Severe hepatotoxicity may be associated with butterbur use, but the evidence is unclear. From the World Health Organization's Vigibase, one study reported 40 cases of hepatotoxicity, including two liver transplants associated with the use of Petasites formulations. [3] However, these cases may be the result of the use of butterbur contaminated with alkaloids. Another article reported detecting levels of toxic alkaloids in seven out of 21 commercially available compounds. [3] Therefore, at least some brands are inadequately removing alkaloids from their butterbur formulations. In summary, it is unclear if hepatotoxicity is due to alkaloids in the formulation or the butterbur itself. To evaluate the hepatotoxic effects of butterbur more clearly, a future study can collect data on brands that claim to be alkaloid-free by thoroughly reviewing the literature and the Periodic Update Safety Report. The data can then be analyzed with the Roussel Uclaf Causality Assessment Method test to determine if butterbur carries an association with hepatotoxicity. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Karbenoxolon is classified as a plant agent product, indicating that it originates from or is derived from botanical sources. Buprenorfin is identified both as a naturally occurring alkaloid and as a plant agent product, showing that it belongs to the class of bioactive compounds produced by plants and also fits within the broader category of plant‑derived agents. Together, these relationships illustrate that plant agent products encompass a range of substances, including synthetic‑type agents such as karbenoxolon and natural alkaloids like buprenorfin, highlighting the importance of plant‑based classifications in pharmacology and drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
retrieved_docs.content: Plant agent product is a classification that includes both karbenoxolon and buprenorfin. Karbenoxolon is identified as a member of the terpeny family, indicating that it is a terpenoid compound derived from plant sources. Buprenorfin, while also a plant agent product, is further characterized as a naturally occurring alkaloid, reflecting its origin from plant alkaloid biosynthesis. In addition to these relationships, ingenol mebutate is recognized as a terpeny, placing it within the same chemical class as karbenoxolon, and it is listed as a member of the “Other chemotherapeutics in ATC” group, indicating its inclusion among therapeutic agents classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical system. Together, these connections illustrate how karbenoxolon and ingenol mebutate share terpenoid chemistry, while buprenorfin bridges the categories of plant‑derived agents and natural alkaloids, and all three compounds are relevant to pharmacological classification and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 372
retrieved_docs.content: Karbenoxolon is identified as a plant‑derived agent that belongs to the terpeny class of compounds, indicating that it is a type of terpenoid produced by plants. Buprenorfin (buprenorphine) is described both as a naturally occurring alkaloid and as a plant‑derived agent product, linking it to the same botanical origin as karbenoxolon. In clinical practice, buprenorfin is employed for the maintenance treatment of opioid dependence, providing long‑term therapy for patients who use opioid substances. The shared classification of these substances as plant‑based agents underscores the relevance of botanical sources in the development of pharmacologically active compounds used for both therapeutic and research purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 857
retrieved_docs.content: Karbenoxolon is identified as a plant agent product and is classified within the terpene family. Buprenorfin is recognized as a naturally occurring alkaloid and is also categorized as a plant agent product. These classifications highlight how both karbenoxolon and buprenorfin are derived from plant sources and belong to distinct chemical families—terpenes for karbenoxolon and alkaloids for buprenorfin—underscoring the diversity of bioactive plant compounds relevant to pharmacology and therapeutic research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2558
retrieved_docs.content: Buprenorfin is classified as a naturally occurring alkaloid, indicating that it is a bioactive chemical compound derived from natural sources with the typical structural features of alkaloids. It is also identified as a plant agent product, emphasizing its origin from botanical material and its role as a plant‑derived substance. Additionally, buprenorfin is described as a phytonutrient, suggesting that it possesses nutritional or therapeutic properties associated with plant‑based nutrients. Recognizing buprenorfin’s overlapping identities as an alkaloid, a plant‑derived product, and a phytonutrient provides a comprehensive understanding of its potential pharmacological effects and its relevance in both medicinal and nutritional contexts.

Within this taxane class, docetaxel is identified as a member, representing one of the specific agents that exemplify the therapeutic application of taxane antineoplastic compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1076
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs used in opioid dependence comprise buprenorfin, levomethadone and lofexidine. Buprenorfin is additionally classified as a naturally occurring alkaloid and as a plant‑derived agent product, indicating its origin from botanical sources. Both lofexidine and beta‑funaltrexamine are identified as narcotic antagonists, reflecting their role in blocking opioid receptor activity. This collection of agents illustrates that medications for opioid dependence span several pharmacologic categories, including natural alkaloids, synthetic antagonists and other agents, and that recognizing these classifications is important for selecting appropriate treatment strategies in opioid use disorder.

Terpeny are plant‑derived chemical compounds that serve as agents in various pharmacological and therapeutic applications. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
retrieved_docs.content: Capsicum is closely linked to capsaicin, the pungent compound that gives the plant its characteristic heat. Capsaicin serves as the parent molecule for the Solanaceae alkaloids, indicating that it is a fundamental chemical precursor within this family of plant-derived alkaloids. In pharmacological classification, capsaicin is regarded both as a biological toxin and as an antipruritic agent, reflecting its dual role as a potentially harmful substance and as a therapeutic compound used to alleviate itching. Together, these connections illustrate how the active ingredient of Capsicum fits into broader chemical families and therapeutic categories, informing both toxicological assessment and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1266
retrieved_docs.content: Trabektedin is a classification that encompasses both DNA minor groove binding agents and other plant alkaloids and natural products listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. As a DNA minor groove binding agent, the compound interacts specifically with the narrow groove of the DNA double helix, influencing processes such as transcription and replication. In addition, the broader group of other plant alkaloids and natural products in ATC that are members of trabektedin includes a variety of biologically active substances derived from plants, highlighting the diverse pharmacological potential within this category. Together, these entities illustrate how trabektedin serves as a unifying group for agents that target DNA structure and for a range of plant‑derived natural products used in therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 708

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: A [Preparations] is a group of medicinal preparations that is linked to the alkaloid ajmalicine and the preparation alseroxylon as its parent sources, indicating that both ajmalicine and alseroxylon serve as foundational compounds or origins for this category. In turn, A [Preparations] functions as the parent of the preparation ankurd, making ankurd a derivative or sub‑preparation within the same lineage. This hierarchical relationship illustrates how ajmalicine and alseroxylon contribute to the development of A [Preparations], which subsequently gives rise to ankurd, thereby clarifying the pharmacological ancestry and potential therapeutic connections among these related botanical or alkaloid‑based medicines.
